title: Term vs. Whole Life Insurance: Which Is Right for You?
seo_title: Term vs Whole Life Insurance Canada | Which Is Better? | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Compare term life insurance vs. whole life insurance in Canada. See real costs, pros and cons, and find out which type fits your situation. Ontario-licensed advisors.

secondary_keywords: term life vs permanent insurance canada
which is better term or whole life
term insurance vs permanent insurance ontario
whole life vs term life cost
_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — after reading comparison, reader chooses a type and requests quotes through PolicyScanner
_meta.page_role: High-value comparison page — often the final research step before buying. Sits between the term and whole-life pillar pages. Links to both.
_meta.writer_focus: Balanced but helpful. Most Canadians should buy term life. Be honest about this while explaining who benefits from whole life. Don't dismiss whole life — it has a place in estate planning and for certain needs. But for income replacement during working years: term wins on cost.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'term vs whole life insurance' 2–3 times per 500 words. Use 'term life' and 'whole life' individually much more often.
synonyms: term insurance vs whole life policy
action_keywords: choose between term and whole life
compare term and whole life costs
natural_variations: deciding between term and whole life
is whole life worth the cost
how term and whole life compare in Canada
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Ahmed, 34, Ottawa — budget $150/month. Term: $1.5M 20-year for $110/month. Whole life: $200K for $150/month. He chose term + invested the $40/month difference.
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not imply whole life is a scam or a bad product. It serves a real purpose for high-net-worth estate planning and specific permanent needs. Be balanced.

page_data.heading: Term vs. Whole Life Insurance: The Honest Comparison
page_data.subheading: Two very different products. Different costs, different purposes, different ideal buyers. Here's how to decide which one you need.

_writer_notes.focus: Neutral, thoughtful intro. This page serves people who are genuinely undecided. Don't sell — educate.
key_points: Term: temporary, lower cost, pure protection
Whole life: permanent, higher cost, builds cash value

page_data.heading: The Short Answer (If You're in a Hurry)

_writer_notes.focus: Give readers the direct answer upfront for those who just want guidance, not a wall of text.
key_points: Choose term life if: you need income/mortgage protection for a defined period (10–30 years), want maximum coverage per dollar, are under 55, have a mortgage or dependents

Choose whole life if: you have a permanent need (estate planning, business insurance, final expenses for life), high net worth wanting tax-sheltered growth, you've maxed RRSP/TFSA and want another tax vehicle
Most Canadians: term life is the right choice
Note: you can have both — term for income protection + small whole life for estate planning

page_data.heading: The Cost Difference: Term Is Much Cheaper

_writer_notes.focus: Real cost comparison. Same person, same coverage amount — term vs whole life.
key_points: Ahmed, 34, male, non-smoker: $150/month budget
Term (20-year): up to $1.5M in coverage for ~$110/month
Whole life: ~$200K coverage for $150/month
Same monthly budget = 7.5x more coverage with term
But whole life premiums stay the same forever; term premiums reset at renewal
'Buy term and invest the difference' — investing $40/month over 20 years at 7% growth = ~$20K
_writer_notes.structure: Side-by-side cost table for same person at ages 30, 35, 40, 45. Then Ahmed's example.

page_data.heading: What Exactly Is Term Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Concise definition. You know your template — don't repeat full term guide here. Just core concept + key facts.
key_points: Coverage for a fixed period (10, 20, 30 years)
Premiums are level for the term period, then jump steeply at renewal
No cash value — pure insurance
If you die during the term: full benefit paid. If you outlive it: coverage ends, no refund (unless ROP rider)
Best for: income replacement, mortgage protection, family protection during working years

page_data.heading: What Exactly Is Whole Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Concise definition. Cover the cash value component honestly — it's a key differentiator.
key_points: Coverage for life — as long as you pay premiums, you're covered
Builds cash value over time — can borrow against it or surrender for cash
Participating: earns dividends (non-guaranteed) on top of guaranteed growth
Premiums are level forever (no renewal surprise)
More complex: paid-up additions, policy loans, surrender values
Best for: estate planning, permanent needs, max funded policies as tax shelter

page_data.heading: The Cash Value Debate: Is It Worth It?

_writer_notes.focus: Honest exploration of the 'whole life as investment' question. Neither dismiss nor over-hype.
key_points: Cash value grows tax-deferred inside the policy — a legitimate benefit
Growth rate: typically 4–6% on participating policies (not guaranteed on the dividend portion)
Accessible: policy loans, partial withdrawals, or full surrender
But: takes years to become significant. First 5–7 years of premiums mostly cover insurance costs
'Buy term and invest the difference': if you invest the premium difference in a diversified portfolio, you'll likely accumulate more wealth
Exception: if you've maxed RRSP and TFSA and need another tax-sheltered growth vehicle — whole life fills that role

page_data.heading: Who Should Buy Term Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Clear profile of ideal term buyer.
key_points: Young families protecting income and a mortgage
Anyone with dependents under age 22
People with significant debt (mortgage, business loan)
Middle-income earners needing maximum coverage for their budget
Anyone who would struggle to maintain large whole life premiums long-term
_writer_notes.callout_after: Link to term life insurance hub page.

page_data.heading: Who Should Buy Whole Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Clear profile of ideal whole life buyer. Be honest — it's a smaller group.
key_points: High-net-worth Canadians with estate tax planning needs
Business owners using life insurance as a key-person or buy-sell tool
People who require permanent coverage (e.g., a dependent who will always need care)
Investors who have maxed RRSP/TFSA and want a third tax-sheltered vehicle
Older adults (55+) who want guaranteed final expense coverage for life
_writer_notes.callout_after: Link to whole life insurance hub page.

page_data.heading: Term vs. Whole Life: Full Comparison Table

_writer_notes.focus: Master comparison table. 10+ rows covering every dimension readers care about.
key_points: Coverage period: Fixed term (T) / Permanent (W)
Monthly cost: Lower (T) / Higher (T, usually 5–10x more per dollar of coverage)
Cash value: No (T) / Yes, grows over time (W)
Premium changes: Stable during term, jumps at renewal (T) / Level forever (W)
Flexibility: Can cancel anytime, low lock-in (T) / Long-term commitment (W)
Best for: Income/debt protection (T) / Estate planning, permanent need (W)
Tax benefits: Payout is tax-free (both) / Cash value grows tax-deferred (W)
Convertibility: Many term policies can convert to permanent (T+W bridge option)
_writer_notes.structure: Two-column table. Bold the row headers. Keep cell content to one short phrase.

page_data.heading: Term vs. Whole Life — Frequently Asked Questions

faqs.question: Can I switch from term to whole life insurance?
faqs.answer: Many term policies include a conversion option that lets you convert to a permanent policy (whole life or universal life) before a certain age, without a new medical exam. This is valuable if your health changes. Check your policy for conversion privileges before your deadline.
faqs._guidance: Explain conversion as a bridge — it reassures term buyers they have flexibility.

faqs.question: Is whole life insurance a good investment in Canada?
faqs.answer: Whole life insurance is not a pure investment — it's insurance with a savings component. The internal rate of return on cash value is typically 3–5% on participating policies. For most Canadians, a diversified RRSP or TFSA will outperform whole life as an investment vehicle. However, for high-net-worth individuals who've maxed registered accounts, whole life offers unique tax advantages.
faqs._guidance: Answer honestly. Don't dismiss whole life as an investment, but don't oversell it either.

faqs.question: What happens when a term life policy expires?
faqs.answer: When your term ends, coverage stops. You'll typically receive a renewal offer at a much higher premium (reflecting your current age). You can renew, convert to permanent (if conversion privilege exists), or let it lapse. Most people who no longer have dependents or a mortgage simply let it lapse.
faqs._guidance: Be direct about what happens at renewal — many people don't understand this.

faqs.question: Can I have both term and whole life insurance?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this is a common strategy. Many Canadians buy a large term policy for income/mortgage protection during their working years, plus a small participating whole life policy for permanent estate planning needs. This 'layered' approach balances cost and long-term coverage.
faqs._guidance: Validate the 'have both' strategy — it's actually common among moderate-to-high earners.
